request.question: Are Jon Fratelli and Skye Edwards from the same country?

title: Jon Fratelli
Morcheeba
Skye Edwards
The Fratellis
Codeine Velvet Club
Chelsea Dagger
Charles Reeves
Eyes Wide, Tongue Tied
A Heady Tale
Psycho Jukebox
sentences: Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland) is a Scottish musician and songwriter best known for his work with the band The Fratellis.
He has also played in a band called Codeine Velvet Club, and also performed as a solo artist.
Morcheeba are an English electronic band formed in the mid 1990s with founding members vocalist Skye Edwards and the brothers Paul and Ross Godfrey.
They mix influences from trip hop, rock, folk-rock and downtempo, and have produced eight regular studio albums since 1995, two of which reached the UK top ten.
Their last studio album, "Head Up High", was released in October 2013.
Edwards left the band in 2003, after which the brothers used a number of singers before she rejoined in 2009.
They recruit additional members for their live performances and have toured internationally.
In 2014 Paul Godfrey resigned from the band, thus ending Morcheeba.
Edwards and Ross Godfrey later formed Skye|Ross and released a self-titled album in September 2016.
They continue to tour, on occasion being listed as "Morcheeba / Sky & Ross Trio".
Skye Edwards (born Shirley Klaris Yonavieve Edwards; 27 May 1974) is a British singer-songwriter.
Her career began in 1994 when she and the Godfrey brothers (Paul Godfrey, a DJ, and Ross Godfrey, a multiinstrumentalist) formed Morcheeba, which released five albums with Skye as lead vocalist.
In 2003, the band split, after which Skye released two solo albums: "Mind How You Go" in 2006, and "Keeping Secrets" in 2009.
In 2010, Edwards returned to Morcheeba, again as lead vocalist.
In 2012, she released her third solo album, "Back to Now".
Her fourth solo album "In A Low Light" was released in 2015.
The Fratellis are a Scottish rock band from Glasgow, formed in 2005.

The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Jon Fratelli (born John Lawler), bass guitarist Barry Fratelli (born Barry Wallace), and drummer and backing vocalist Mince Fratelli (born Gordon McRory).
Their singles "Chelsea Dagger" and "Whistle for the Choir" were both top ten hits in the UK charts.
Codeine Velvet Club was a Scottish alternative rock band formed in 2008 by Lou Hickey and Jon Lawler, a.k.a. Jon Fratelli from The Fratellis.
The band's debut album "Codeine Velvet Club" was released on 28 December 2009 in the UK and on 6 April 2010 in the US.
"Chelsea Dagger" is a song by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.
It was released as the second single from their debut studio album, "Costello Music" (2006), on 28 August 2006.
It is named after Jon Fratelli's wife Heather, a burlesque dancer whose stage name – a play on Britney Spears – he borrowed for the song.
Fratelli described the tune as "a rock 'n' roll gig in an old speakeasy or something like that."

Charles "Chicky" Reeves is a producer, recording, mixing and mastering engineer working with famous artists such as Prince, UB40, Ray Charles, Tito Puente, Johnny Cash, and Radiohead—as well as MTV's Live at the Ten Spot, VH1 Storytellers, Boyz II Men, Apollo Heights, D-Lite, 247Spys.
In 2004, Reeves moved to London with his New-York-schooled-style.

He continues to collaborate with artists in the studio as well as mixing front of house for Orchestral Manoeuvres in the Dark, Grace Jones, Francesca Longrigg, Scarlett Etienne, Dana Boulé, BEF (formerly Heaven 17), Onetwo, Skye Edwards (of Morcheeba), Prince, Rachael Cormack, UB40, and the Icelandic band Trabant.
His studio, Sublime Studios, is located in Islington, London, England.
His music is published by Extreme Music.
Eyes Wide, Tongue Tied is the fourth album by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.
The album was revealed on 1 June 2015, alongside the first track "Me and the Devil" (which was given away for free from the bands website), and released on 21 August 2015.

The album was written and recorded in Los Angeles, California during November 2014 with Tony Hoffer, who also produced the band's debut album "Costello Music" and Jon Fratelli's solo album "Psycho Jukebox".
"A Heady Tale" is the third single by Scottish rock band, The Fratellis from their second album, "Here We Stand".
It was released on 22 December 2008.
This song is 1 of the 4 tracks on "Here We Stand" to have a heavy use of piano (the other 3 being "Mistress Mabel", "Milk And Money" & "Moriaty's Last Stand").
The B-sides "Mon Yous, Mon us but no' Them" and "When All the Lights Go Out" were also piano intensive.
Jon Fratelli has said that the B-Side "Lonesome Anti Christmas Blues" is the "most depressing Christmas song written".
Psycho Jukebox is the debut solo album by Jon Fratelli, frontman of The Fratellis and formerly of Codeine Velvet Club.
Released on 25 July 2011.
It was recorded with Tony Hoffer (who produced "Costello Music" and mixed "Codeine Velvet Club") in the Sound Factory studios in Los Angeles.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Are Jon Fratelli and Skye Edwards from the same country?
hop1_docs: The Fratellis | The Fratellis are a Scottish rock band from Glasgow, formed in 2005.  The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Jon Fratelli (born John Lawler), bass guitarist Barry Fratelli (born Barry Wallace), and drummer and backing vocalist Mince Fratelli (born Gordon McRory).  Their singles "Chelsea Dagger" and "Whistle for the Choir" were both top ten hits in the UK charts.

Codeine Velvet Club | Codeine Velvet Club was a Scottish alternative rock band formed in 2008 by Lou Hickey and Jon Lawler, a.k.a. Jon Fratelli from The Fratellis.  The band's debut album "Codeine Velvet Club" was released on 28 December 2009 in the UK and on 6 April 2010 in the US.

Jon Fratelli | Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland) is a Scottish musician and songwriter best known for his work with the band The Fratellis.  He has also played in a band called Codeine Velvet Club, and also performed as a solo artist.

Chelsea Dagger | "Chelsea Dagger" is a song by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.  It was released as the second single from their debut studio album, "Costello Music" (2006), on 28 August 2006.  It is named after Jon Fratelli's wife Heather, a burlesque dancer whose stage name – a play on Britney Spears – he borrowed for the song.  Fratelli described the tune as "a rock 'n' roll gig in an old speakeasy or something like that."

Skye-Jilly Edwards | Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder who won the Miss Globe International 1994 in Istanbul, Turkey and represented her country in the Miss World 1994 pageant in Sun City, South Africa.  She was rated in the top 50 models in Australia by Ralph Magazine.

Psycho Jukebox | Psycho Jukebox is the debut solo album by Jon Fratelli, frontman of The Fratellis and formerly of Codeine Velvet Club.  Released on 25 July 2011.  It was recorded with Tony Hoffer (who produced "Costello Music" and mixed "Codeine Velvet Club") in the Sound Factory studios in Los Angeles.

The Skye Boat Song | "The Skye Boat Song" is a Scottish folk song, which can be played as a waltz, recalling the escape of Prince Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie) from Uist to the Isle of Skye after his defeat at the Battle of Culloden in 1746.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Jon Fratelli: passage [3] ("Jon Fratelli") — quote: "born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland". Also passage [1] ("The Fratellis") — quote: "Scottish rock band from Glasgow".
  - Skye Edwards: passage [5] ("Skye-Jilly Edwards") — quote: "Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian".
- Ambiguities:
  - The question uses the name "Skye Edwards" but the passages contain "Skye-Jilly Edwards". It is unclear if "Skye Edwards" refers to the same "Skye-Jilly Edwards" page or to a different person (no plain "Skye Edwards" page appears in the provided passages).
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as given in passages): "Skye-Jilly Edwards".
  - Precise missing fact needed: confirmation of which "Skye Edwards" is intended and that person's country/nationality (i.e., whether the intended Skye's country matches Jon Fratelli's country).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open "Skye-Jilly Edwards" page — check the lede/infobox ("Born"/"Nationality") to confirm "Australian" (passage [5] already suggests this).
     - Search hint: "Skye-Jilly Edwards lede infobox nationality".
  2) Open "Skye Edwards" (disambiguation) or the plain "Skye Edwards" page — check lede/infobox to see if a different person (with a different country) is intended.
     - Search hint: "Skye Edwards disambiguation" or "Skye Edwards page nationality".
  3) Re-open "Jon Fratelli" page lede/infobox (or section 'Born') to re-confirm birthplace/nationality ("Glasgow, Scotland"/"Scottish") if needed.
     - Search hint: "Jon Fratelli lede infobox born Glasgow Scotland".
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Skye-Jilly Edwards | Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder who won the Miss Globe International 1994 in Istanbul, Turkey and represented her country in the Miss World 1994 pageant in Sun City, South Africa.  She was rated in the top 50 models in Australia by Ralph Magazine.

Skye Edwards | Skye Edwards (born Shirley Klaris Yonavieve Edwards; 27 May 1974) is a British singer-songwriter.  Her career began in 1994 when she and the Godfrey brothers (Paul Godfrey, a DJ, and Ross Godfrey, a multiinstrumentalist) formed Morcheeba, which released five albums with Skye as lead vocalist.  In 2003, the band split, after which Skye released two solo albums: "Mind How You Go" in 2006, and "Keeping Secrets" in 2009.  In 2010, Edwards returned to Morcheeba, again as lead vocalist.  In 2012, she released her third solo album, "Back to Now".  Her fourth solo album "In A Low Light" was released in 2015.

The Skye Boat Song | "The Skye Boat Song" is a Scottish folk song, which can be played as a waltz, recalling the escape of Prince Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie) from Uist to the Isle of Skye after his defeat at the Battle of Culloden in 1746.

Mind How You Go (Skye Edwards album) | Mind How You Go is Skye Edwards's debut solo album, following her departure from Morcheeba.  Despite anticipation by fans, the album achieved limited success in the UK as a result of insufficient promotion.  It was more successful in other parts of Europe, particularly Poland, France, Italy and Switzerland.
Jilly | Jilly is a feminine given name.  Notable people with the name include:
Jilly Rizzo | Ermenigildo "Jilly" Rizzo (May 6, 1917 – May 6, 1992) was an American restaurateur and entertainer.
Jilly Johnson | Jilly Johnson (née Gosden) (born 17 November 1953) is a former "Page 3" girl.
hop2_titles: Mind How You Go (Skye Edwards album)
response.summary_2: Not definitively — the passages show different nationalities for the names involved. Jon Fratelli: born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland. Skye-Jilly Edwards: is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder; Skye Edwards: (born Shirley Klaris Yonavieve Edwards; 27 May 1974) is a British singer-songwriter.
response.answer: Ambiguous — Skye Edwards is listed as Australian (Skye-Jilly) or British (singer), while Jon Fratelli is Scottish.
